# Welcome {.unnumbered}

This course teaches statistics the way research actually works starting
with your data, not a textbook formula. Every method is introduced with
a practical scenario, explained in plain language, and then
implemented step-by-step in R.

The course is designed for PhD students in biomedical and clinical
research. That includes people working with human patient data, animal
experiments, survey data, or longitudinal studies. You do not need to be
an expert in R or statistics to start, but you do need R and RStudio
installed on your computer.

::: callout-important
### Start Here: Install R and Course Packages

Before opening any session, go to [**Before You Begin:
Setup**](#sec-setup) and follow the setup steps there. You will install
R, RStudio, and all the packages the course uses. This takes about
10--15 minutes and only needs to be done once.
:::

## How the Course Is Organized {.unnumbered}

The course has four parts. Each part builds on the previous one.

::: callout-tip
### Choose Your Starting Point

| Your background                                     | Where to start                                                     |
|-----------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------|
| New to statistics                                   | **Part 1** work through the course in order                        |
| Familiar with R, new to modeling                    | **Part 1** then continue to Part 2                                 |
| Interested in regression and prediction             | **Part 2** after reviewing Part 1 if needed                       |
| Looking for advanced methods only                   | **Part 3** after the core concepts in Parts 1 and 2               |
| Need a quick method guide                           | **Part 4** for the reference and resources                        |

If you are unsure where to begin, start with Part 1 and follow the
course sequentially.
:::

::: callout-tip
### Your Quick Reference

The **Statistical Decision Guide** in Part 4 is a reference card you can
return to at any point. It tells you which method to use for common
research questions and links back to the relevant sessions.
:::

### Part 1: Inference — Testing and Estimation {.unnumbered}

This part introduces the foundations of statistical inference and
hypothesis testing. You will learn how to interpret p-values, confidence
intervals, and effect sizes before moving on to regression and modeling.

-   Sampling, confidence intervals, and the central limit theorem
-   Hypothesis testing and the logic of evidence
-   t-tests for comparing means
-   Non-parametric alternatives when assumptions fail
-   ANOVA for comparing multiple groups
-   Categorical tests such as chi-square and Fisher's exact
-   Power and sample size planning

### Part 2: Regression and Modeling {.unnumbered}

This part covers the most commonly used predictive models in biomedical
research. You will learn how to fit models, interpret coefficients,
check assumptions, and report results clearly.

-   Correlation and association
-   Linear regression for continuous outcomes
-   Multiple regression for adjusted effects
-   Logistic regression for binary outcomes
-   Model building, diagnostics, and validation
-   Missing data handling with multiple imputation
-   Mixed models for repeated measures and clustered data

### Part 3: Advanced Methods {.unnumbered}

This part introduces advanced analytic methods for more complex study
designs and causal questions.

-   Survival analysis and time-to-event data
-   Causal inference using DAGs and observational data
-   Mendelian randomization for genetic instrumental variables

### Part 4: Reference and Resources {.unnumbered}

This part gives you tools to apply the methods thoughtfully and find
answers when you need them.

-   Statistical Decision Guide for method selection
-   Clinical research methods and study design considerations
-   Help and documentation for using the course materials

## Prerequisites {.unnumbered}

-   R and RStudio installed on your computer
-   Basic ability to run R code in RStudio
-   No prior statistics knowledge is assumed beyond high-school level

## How to Use This Course {.unnumbered}

Each session follows the same structure:

1.  **When do you use this?** A concrete scenario from your field
2.  **Background** Why the method works, in plain language
3.  **Worked examples** Step-by-step R code on real data
4.  **What can go wrong** Common mistakes and how to avoid them
5.  **Exercises** Practice problems with solutions
6.  **Comprehension check** Self-check questions

You can work through the course week by week, or jump to a specific
method when you need it. If you are unsure which method to use, go to
the [Statistical Decision
Guide](sessions/statistical-decision-guide.qmd) in Part 4.
